1: A brief regnal formula for Zedekiah — מַלַּךְ (mallakh, was made king): the passive/causative points to Nebuchadnezzar as the appointing power (Jeremiah 37:1b makes this explicit). תַּחַת (tachath, in place of): Coniah/Jeconiah was deposed and exiled (597 BCE); Zedekiah rules as a vassal king, not a free monarch. The opening frames everything that follows: a king installed by Babylon is now secretly consulting the prophet to resist Babylon.
2: לֹא שָׁמְעוּ (lo shame'u, they did not listen): the Jeremianic indictment verb — shama' (listen, obey) or its negation is the hinge between covenant obedience and covenant disaster throughout the book. All three parties are indicted: the king, his servants (avadav), and the people of the land (am ha'aretz) — no class is exempt. The words were spoken through (beyad, by the hand of) Jeremiah — the prophetic instrumentality formula.
3: דְּרַשׁ (drash, inquire/seek): Zedekiah's sending of envoys to Jeremiah is an act of cultic consultation — drash is the technical verb for seeking a divine oracle. The irony is acute: the king who refuses to heed Jeremiah's sustained preaching still treats him as an oracular resource to be consulted in a crisis. הִתְפַּלֵּל (hitpallel, pray): the request is for intercessory prayer — YHWH is approached through the prophet's mediation.
4: בָּא וְיֹצֵא (ba veyotze', coming and going): Jeremiah's freedom of movement is noted because it is about to end. The circumstantial clause explains the state of affairs before the main action of the chapter. The author's literary technique: establish the prophet's relative freedom, then show how it is taken away by those he is trying to warn.
5: Pharaoh's army (cheil Par'oh) advances and the Chaldeans lift the siege temporarily. שָׁמְעוּ (same'u, they heard): the same root used in v2 for Israel's failure to hear — the Chaldeans hear military intelligence and respond; Israel hears the word of YHWH and does not respond. The contrast is built into the vocabulary.
6: The characteristic prophetic messenger formula: the word of YHWH came (vayehi devar YHWH el) — the initiative is entirely divine; the prophet is a receiver and transmitter, not an originator. The oracle in vv7-10 is YHWH's direct reply to Zedekiah's request for a word.
7: שֹׁב יָשׁוּב (shov yashuv, he will return): the infinitive absolute for emphasis — Pharaoh's army will certainly return to Egypt; its departure is not permanent rescue. The same shuv root that means 'repent/return' throughout Jeremiah is used here in its literal sense, but the irony is present: what Israel fails to do (turn/repent to YHWH), Pharaoh's army does naturally (return to its own land).
8: וְלָכְדוּהָ וּשְׂרָפוּהָ בָאֵשׁ (velakheduha userafuha va'esh, they will capture it and burn it with fire): the dual fate of the city is stated with unambiguous finality. The burning with fire (esh) is a recurring prophetic image for Jerusalem's destruction (cf. Jeremiah 21:10, Jeremiah 32:29, Jeremiah 38:18) and the fire of YHWH's covenant judgment (Deuteronomy 32:22).
9: אַל-תַּשִּׁאוּ נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם (al-tashi'u nafshoteykhem, do not deceive yourselves): the reflexive idiom — literally 'do not lift up your own lives [in vain hope].' The warning against self-deception is psychologically precise: under siege pressure, people generate false hope from any temporary relief. Halokh yelekhu (they will certainly go away) uses emphatic infinitive absolute — the Chaldeans are definitely not leaving permanently.
10: The argument reaches extreme hypothetical: even if only wounded men (anashim medukkaim) were left from the Chaldean army, they would still rise and burn the city. The point is theological: the judgment is YHWH's own determination and cannot be deflected by military events. Human calculations are irrelevant when divine decree is operative.
11: A narrative transition verse — הֵעָלוֹת חֵיל הַכַּשְׂדִּים (he'alot cheil hakhashdim, when the Chaldean army had withdrawn/gone up): the siege is lifted. The vocabulary of military withdrawal sets the scene for Jeremiah's attempt to leave the city on private business.
12: חָלַק מִשָּׁם (chalaq misham, to receive his portion there): the unusual phrase may mean receiving a hereditary property division or share. Anathoth was Jeremiah's hometown (Jeremiah 1:1). The mundane detail — the prophet trying to conduct personal property business during a crisis — is characteristic of Jeremiah's narrative: his life and the city's fate are constantly intertwined. בְּנִיָמִן (Binyamin, Benjamin): Anathoth was in the tribal territory of Benjamin.
13: נֹפֵל אַתָּה אֶל-הַכַּשְׂדִּים (nofel attah el-hakhashdim, you are defecting/falling to the Chaldeans): nafal el (fall/go over to) is the idiomatic expression for defection, used throughout Jeremiah 37-39. The arrest is based on political suspicion, not legal evidence — Irijah does not listen (lo shama', again the indictment verb) to Jeremiah's denial.
14: שֶׁקֶר (sheqer, lie/falsehood): Jeremiah's denial uses the same word that characterizes the false prophets throughout the book. The irony: the only true prophet accuses his accusers of falsehood, while the false prophets are never arrested. Irijah refuses to listen — the echo of v2 (the people refused to listen) is intentional.
15: הִכּוּ (hikku, they beat him): the officials' violence against the prophet is unambiguous. בֵּית הָאֵסוּר (beit ha'esur, house of detention/imprisonment): literally 'house of the bond/chain.' The scribal house of Jonathan is converted into a prison — the institution of literacy (the scribe) becomes the instrument of prophetic suppression.
16: חֲנֻיֹּות הַבּוֹר (chanuyot habor, the vaulted cells of the pit): bor (pit/cistern) is Jeremiah's prison vocabulary — the same word appears in Jeremiah 38's cistern narrative. The pit (bor) is also the underworld, the place of the dead in Psalms and elsewhere. The prison conditions approximate death — a motif that will be made literal in Jeremiah 38.
17: הֲיֵשׁ דָּבָר מֵאֵת יְהוָה (hayesh davar me'et YHWH, is there a word from YHWH?): Zedekiah's private inquiry is urgent and genuine — he wants to know YHWH's word. Jeremiah's reply is direct: יֵשׁ (yesh, there is). The answer: 'You will be handed over to the king of Babylon.' The word from YHWH has not changed since Jeremiah 21. No imprisonment changes the prophetic word.
18: Jeremiah's legal complaint (ma chatatati lekha, what have I sinned against you): the language is formal legal protestation of innocence. He inverts the question — if his prophecy was false, what specific wrong did he do? The implicit argument: prophecy that accurately describes what is happening cannot be criminalized.
19: הַנֹּבְאִים לָכֶם (hannov'im lakhem, the prophets who prophesied to you): the false prophets who promised 'the king of Babylon will not come against you' are rhetorically invoked. Their non-appearance before the enemy is proof of their falsehood — where are they now? The prophetic vindication comes through historical fulfillment, not institutional endorsement.
20: תִּפֹּל תַּחֲנוּנָתִי (tipol tachanunatiy, let my plea fall before you): tachanunim are earnest supplications/entreaties — the word used in prayers seeking mercy. The request not to be returned to Jonathan's prison is the only personal request Jeremiah makes in the entire chapter. The prophet who has given YHWH's word faithfully asks only for basic survival.
21: חֲצַר הַמַּטָּרָה (chatzer hamattarah, court of the guard): the less severe confinement — an open courtyard detention rather than a dungeon cell. כִּכַּר לֶחֶם (kikkar lechem, loaf of bread): the daily provision from the bakers' street (chutz ha'ofim) until the bread ran out. The narrator preserves the physical detail — the prophet of divine judgment is kept alive by a daily bread allotment, a small mercy in the catastrophe.
